title: Advanced Path Traversal with Null Byte
description: An application that appends .pdf to the filename and is running on an older version of Python/C that is vulnerable to null byte injection.
vuln_code:
filename = request.args.get('name')
path = f"./documents/{filename}.pdf"
with open(path, "rb") as f:
    return f.read()

stages.name: Find the vulnerability
stages.instruction: Identify how an attacker can bypass the '.pdf' extension enforcement.
stages.target: An attacker can use a null byte (%00) to terminate the string in the underlying C call, effectively ignoring the '.pdf' extension.

stages.name: Exploit it
stages.instruction: Provide a payload to read the /etc/passwd file, bypassing the .pdf extension.

stages.name: Patch it
stages.instruction: Besides updating the environment, how can you validate that the filename doesn't contain null bytes or other dangerous characters?
stages.target: Check if '\0' is in the filename string, or use a regex to only allow alphanumeric characters and a single dot.

stages.name: Explain it
stages.instruction: Explain why the null byte (%00) was historically effective in bypassing file extension checks.
stages.target: In C and many other languages, a null byte signifies the end of a string. High-level languages might allow null bytes in strings, but when passed to lower-level C system calls (like open), the string is truncated at the first null byte.

hints: Think about how strings are terminated in the C language.
